findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a periarticular soft tissue mass containing numerous round calcifications with central lucencies, characteristic of phleboliths. This is accompanied by local soft tissue swelling without frank destruction of the underlying bone.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include smooth periosteal reaction or reactive bony changes in the adjacent diaphysis. On MRI, these lesions typically demonstrate prominent serpentine flow voids and avid enhancement, with hyperintense signal on T2-weighted imaging.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of peripheral, mature zonal ossification to suggest myositis ossificans. Furthermore, there is no associated acro-osteolysis, distal tuft resorption, or widespread subcutaneous dystrophic calcifications.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a soft tissue mass containing pathognomonic phleboliths (round calcifications with lucent centers).

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Hemangioma, given the periarticular soft tissue mass with internal phleboliths. These common benign vascular tumors often present in children and young adults with pain during activity, and the presence of phleboliths on plain radiography is virtually diagnostic.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Scleroderma, however this typically presents with sheet-like or nodular subcutaneous dystrophic calcifications (calcinosis cutis) rather than discrete phleboliths within a focal mass. Additionally, scleroderma is usually associated with characteristic distal phalangeal tuft resorption and joint contractures, which are absent here.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Myositis ossiﬁ cans, given it can present as a painful calcifying soft tissue mass following trauma, and misdiagnosing it early on could lead to an unnecessary biopsy that might mimic a sarcoma histologically. However, myositis ossificans classically develops a dense peripheral rim of mature ossification (zoning phenomenon) rather than the scattered phleboliths seen in this case.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to define the full anatomical extent of the vascular malformation and assess its relationship to the adjacent joint, neurovascular bundle, and muscle compartments.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to evaluate the growth rate of the lesion or to identify any prior episodes of thrombosis within the vascular spaces.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the affected limb without and with intravenous contrast, as this modality is superior here for characterizing the high T2 signal of pooling blood, identifying fibrous septa, and mapping the feeding and draining vessels.
management.mriSequences: I would specify fluid-sensitive T2-weighted or STIR sequences to demonstrate the marked hyperintensity typical of slow-flow vascular spaces, alongside T1 pre- and post-contrast sequences to assess the characteristic intense serpentine enhancement and internal flow voids.
management.intervention: If symptomatic relief or surgical resection is planned, I would specify an ultrasound-guided assessment or formal catheter angiography for potential preoperative embolisation to minimize surgical bleeding.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Musculoskeletal and Vascular Anomalies MDT. My report would clearly document the intra- and extramuscular extent of the hemangioma, the presence of any feeding vessels, and the proximity to the adjacent joint capsule.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a periarticular soft tissue mass containing numerous round calcifications with central lucencies, characteristic of phleboliths. This is accompanied by local soft tissue swelling without frank destruction of the underlying bone. Supporting features include smooth periosteal reaction or reactive bony changes in the adjacent diaphysis. On MRI, these lesions typically demonstrate prominent serpentine flow voids and avid enhancement, with hyperintense signal on T2-weighted imaging. Importantly, there is no evidence of peripheral, mature zonal ossification to suggest myositis ossificans. Furthermore, there is no associated acro-osteolysis, distal tuft resorption, or widespread subcutaneous dystrophic calcifications. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a soft tissue mass containing pathognomonic phleboliths (round calcifications with lucent centers).

The appearances are most in keeping with Hemangioma, given the periarticular soft tissue mass with internal phleboliths. These common benign vascular tumors often present in children and young adults with pain during activity, and the presence of phleboliths on plain radiography is virtually diagnostic. The principle differential would be Scleroderma, however this typically presents with sheet-like or nodular subcutaneous dystrophic calcifications (calcinosis cutis) rather than discrete phleboliths within a focal mass. Additionally, scleroderma is usually associated with characteristic distal phalangeal tuft resorption and joint contractures, which are absent here. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Myositis ossiﬁ cans, given it can present as a painful calcifying soft tissue mass following trauma, and misdiagnosing it early on could lead to an unnecessary biopsy that might mimic a sarcoma histologically. However, myositis ossificans classically develops a dense peripheral rim of mature ossification (zoning phenomenon) rather than the scattered phleboliths seen in this case.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to define the full anatomical extent of the vascular malformation and assess its relationship to the adjacent joint, neurovascular bundle, and muscle compartments. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to evaluate the growth rate of the lesion or to identify any prior episodes of thrombosis within the vascular spaces. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the affected limb without and with intravenous contrast, as this modality is superior here for characterizing the high T2 signal of pooling blood, identifying fibrous septa, and mapping the feeding and draining vessels. I would specify fluid-sensitive T2-weighted or STIR sequences to demonstrate the marked hyperintensity typical of slow-flow vascular spaces, alongside T1 pre- and post-contrast sequences to assess the characteristic intense serpentine enhancement and internal flow voids. If symptomatic relief or surgical resection is planned, I would specify an ultrasound-guided assessment or formal catheter angiography for potential preoperative embolisation to minimize surgical bleeding. This should go to the Musculoskeletal and Vascular Anomalies MDT. My report would clearly document the intra- and extramuscular extent of the hemangioma, the presence of any feeding vessels, and the proximity to the adjacent joint capsule.
